front: Poem: 'Love is so short, forgetting is so long'; 'The night is starry and the stars are blue and shiver in the distance'
back: "Tonight I Can Write the Saddest Lines" / Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair (Pablo Neruda)
work: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair

front: Poem: refrains 'in you everything sank!' and 'it is the hour of departure'; opens 'The memory of you emerges from the night around me'; addressee is 'lost discoverer'
back: "The Song of Despair" / Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair (Pablo Neruda)
work: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair

front: Poem: opens 'white hills, white thighs, you look like a world, lying in surrender'
back: "Body of a Woman" / Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair (Pablo Neruda)
work: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair

front: Poem: translated by W. S. Merwin; published when its author was 19; Huidobro accused the poet of plagiarizing Tagore's The Gardener
back: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair (Pablo Neruda)
work: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair

front: Poem: 12-part poem beginning 'From breeze to breeze, like an empty net'; the speaker climbs 'up the ladder of the earth' to an Incan ruin and commands 'Arise to birth with me, my brother'
back: "The Heights of Macchu Picchu" / Canto General (Pablo Neruda)
work: Canto General / The Heights of Macchu Picchu

front: Poem: sections include 'A Lamp on Earth,' 'The Conquistadors,' 'The Sand Betrayed,' 'The Fugitive'; opens with 'The Invisible Man' declaring 'I laugh / I smile / at the old poets'
back: Canto General (Pablo Neruda)
work: Canto General / The Heights of Macchu Picchu

front: Poem: ode to socks knitted by Maru Mori; 'beauty is twice beauty'; socks 'as soft as rabbits'; feet look like 'two long sharks' or 'two immense blackbirds'
back: "Ode to My Socks" / Elemental Odes (Pablo Neruda)

front: Poem: ode to a 'bullet from the ocean depths' lying 'among the market greens'; described as 'catafalqued king' and 'navigating the waters of death'
back: "Ode to a Large Tuna in the Market" / Elemental Odes (Pablo Neruda)

front: Poem: ode to a vegetable 'dressed up like a warrior' that is stripped 'scale by scale' to eat 'the peaceful mush of its green heart'
back: "Ode to the Artichoke" / Elemental Odes (Pablo Neruda)

front: Poem: opens 'It so happens I am sick of being a man'; speaker imagines himself as 'a felt swan navigating in water of sources and ashes'; Federico Garcia Lorca drew images to accompany it
back: "Walking Around" / Residence on Earth (Pablo Neruda)
work: Walking Around / I Explain a Few Things / Residence on Earth

front: Poem: ends 'Come and see the blood in the streets!'; inspired by the Spanish Civil War in Madrid; set 'with bells, and clocks, and trees'
back: "I Explain a Few Things" / Residence on Earth (Pablo Neruda)
work: Walking Around / I Explain a Few Things / Residence on Earth

front: Poem: Sonnet XVII begins 'I don't love you as if you were a rose of salt, topaz'; 'I love you as one loves certain obscure things, secretly, between the shadow and the soul'
back: One Hundred Love Sonnets (Pablo Neruda)

front: Poem: 'if suddenly you forget me / do not look for me / for I already have forgotten you'; 'everything that exists, aromas, light, metals, are little boats that sail toward the isles of the addressee'
back: "If You Forget Me" / The Captain's Verses (Pablo Neruda)

front: Poem: 'deny me bread, air, light, spring / but do not take from me your laughter'
back: "Your Laughter" / The Captain's Verses (Pablo Neruda)

front: Author: always wrote in green ink, which he considered the color of hope
back: Pablo Neruda

front: Author: appears as a character in Antonio Skarmeta's The Postman, giving advice to Mario on Isla Negra; also appears in Roberto Bolano's By Night in Chile
back: Pablo Neruda

front: Author: in his Nobel lecture, rejected Huidobro's creationist slogan 'the poet is a little god'; quoted Rimbaud about 'burning patience'
back: Pablo Neruda

front: Author: the Congress for Cultural Freedom tried to discredit this poet by publicizing pro-Stalin sections of Grapes and the Wind
back: Pablo Neruda

front: Author: born Ricardo Reyes Basoalto; took pen name from Czech poet Jan Neruda
back: Pablo Neruda

front: Author: a proponent of 'impure poetry'; valued 'useful objects in repose' giving 'reality a magnetism that should not be scorned'
back: Pablo Neruda
